quote: I've been interested for, since before I even bought my house.
stewart_read: Georgia volunteers childhood-level solar interest without prompting. This is the deepest interest signal in the verify section — it marks her as a long-consideration lead, not an impulse inquiry. Tyk acknowledges it ('Oh, that's awesome') but does not anchor on it. The schema notes that top setters spend 60+ seconds on the interest question; Tyk moves on in under 10 seconds.
alternative_hypothesis: Tyk may have moved quickly because the call was going smoothly and he wanted to maintain momentum through verify — a reasonable pacing choice on a cooperative lead.
coaching_implication: Show Tyk the schema note on interest_in_solar depth. The childhood framing was a gift — a 15-second anchor ('So this has been something you've been thinking about for years — what finally made you pull the trigger?') would have deepened emotional investment before the utility issue surfaced, making the callback promise stickier.

quote: Looks like you get lots and lots of sunlight, so it shouldn't be an issue at all. Is that an asphalt shingle roof? It looks like it is.
stewart_read: Tyk uses satellite data to confirm sunlight and roof type proactively, without waiting for Georgia to answer. This is efficient verify execution — he is doing the work before the call rather than interrogating the customer. The roof confirmation also implicitly checks the hard-DQ condition (roof age/type) without making it feel like a checklist.
coaching_implication: This is a positive execution moment worth naming in the 1-on-1. Tyk's satellite prep work is exactly the kind of pre-call discipline that keeps verify feeling like a conversation rather than an interrogation. Reinforce it.

quote: Looks like I have you down as the property owner as well. Is there anybody else for the title with you, or is it just you?
stewart_read: Tyk asks the title/co-owner question cleanly and Georgia confirms she is the sole owner. This is correct protocol execution — the question is asked, the answer is captured, and there is no co-decision-maker complication to manage. No coaching gap here; this is a clean beat.
coaching_implication: Another positive execution moment. Tyk hit the co-owner check naturally and early. Worth noting in the 1-on-1 as evidence that his verify instincts are sound — the problem on this call is not verify discipline, it is what happens after verify hits a wall.

stewart_read: Georgia's question — almost certainly 'Are we qualified?' — is a direct signal that she has registered the uncertainty in Tyk's tone and is checking whether she is about to be turned away. This is a qualification-anxiety moment, not a vulnerability disclosure. Tyk does not acknowledge the question before delivering the uncertain news at 03:12. The correct move here is to acknowledge the anxiety first ('I want to make sure we get you the right answer on this') before delivering the utility uncertainty — delivering uncertain news into an unacknowledged anxiety question compounds the unsettled close.
alternative_hypothesis: Tyk may not have heard the question clearly given the audio gap between 02:34 and 02:49 — the call had a significant pause and the question may have been partially cut off.
coaching_implication: Coach Tyk on the sequence: acknowledge the anxiety signal before delivering uncertain news. The line does not need to be elaborate — 'Great question, let me make sure I have the right answer for you' buys two seconds and reframes the news delivery as customer-service rather than rejection. This is a replicable micro-move for any call where a data gap surfaces mid-verify.

quote: It does look like we're not able to work with the utility company. That was updated as of two years ago. So I might need to just check with my design team to make sure we're able to to help you out there.
stewart_read: Tyk reads from what he explicitly acknowledges is a two-year-old exclusion list. The coaching gap here is not that Tyk lacked knowledge — it is that he had stale data and no protocol for handling it. He hedges correctly ('might need to just check') but then closes the call without locking a callback time, without anchoring Georgia's high intent, and without framing the callback as a near-certain resolution rather than an uncertain outcome. The stale-data problem is an operational gap (Ion's utility compatibility list needs a refresh cadence); the no-locked-callback-time problem is a rep execution gap.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Tyk may have deliberately kept the close soft to avoid over-promising on a utility status he genuinely could not confirm — a reasonable instinct. Rejected because the correct move is to lock the callback time regardless of the uncertainty; the uncertainty is about utility status, not about whether to call Georgia back.
coaching_implication: Two separate coaching conversations: (1) With Tyk — when stale data surfaces, the protocol is 'lock the callback time before you hang up.' Suggested direction: 'Let me check with my design team today and call you back — are you available tomorrow at [time]?' The callback promise without a locked time is a structural no-show risk on a warm lead. (2) With the manager — flag the two-year-old utility exclusion list as an operational gap. If Ion's compatibility data is not current, setters will keep losing warm leads to stale DQs.

quote: So I might need to just check with my design team to make sure we're able to to help you out there. Might be an issue with, like, their net metering program. So let me ask them, and then I can give you a callback here shortly.
stewart_read: The callback promise is made but structurally unprotected on three dimensions: (1) no locked time ('here shortly' is not a time), (2) no acknowledgment of Georgia's anxiety question, (3) no anchor back to her high-intent profile to make the callback feel worth waiting for. Georgia's compliance ('Alright. Yeah. That works. Thank you.') is polite, not enthusiastic — the call ends with the lead in an unresolved state.
alternative_hypothesis: Tyk may have judged that locking a specific callback time was premature given he did not yet know when he would have an answer from the design team — a reasonable operational concern. Rejected because the callback time can be set as a 'latest by' anchor ('I'll have an answer for you by tomorrow afternoon — can I reach you at this number around 2pm?') without over-committing on the utility answer itself.
coaching_implication: Use this moment to drill the locked-callback protocol. The manager should role-play the close with Tyk: 'You have a warm lead, you have a data gap, you need to hang up — what do you say?' The target output is a specific day and time, a restatement of Georgia's interest ('You've been thinking about this since you were a kid — I want to make sure we get you the right answer'), and a clear next step. This is a replicable close for any call that hits a mid-verify wall.

quote: I kinda just wanted to look into clean energy.
stewart_read: Georgia adds an environmental motivation layer on top of her childhood interest — she is not purely bill-pain driven. Tyk does not capture this as a second anchor. On a callback, the closer (or Tyk on the callback) has two emotional hooks available: long-held personal conviction AND environmental values. Neither was anchored for handoff.
coaching_implication: Flag for the handoff brief: Georgia's motivation is dual — personal conviction (childhood interest) and environmental values. The callback opener should reference both, not just the utility status update. This is the kind of tieback the schema's text_after_call section requires — it applies equally to a callback promise.
